data.title: REG - Carr's Group PLC - Total Voting Rights
data.description:
RNS Number : 8648K Carr's Group PLC 30 May 2025  

  

30 May 2025

Carr's Group plc (the "Company")

Total Voting Rights

In accordance with the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure and Transparency Rule 5.6.1, the Company's issued share capital now comprises 94,581,523 ordinary shares of 2.5p each.  The Company holds no shares in treasury.

Each ordinary share carries the right to one vote in relation to all circumstances at general meetings of the Company.  The total number of voting rights in the Company is therefore 94,581,523.

The above figure may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the FCA's Disclosure and Transparency Rules.

Enquiries:

Paula Robertson                                                               Tel: +44 (0) 1228 554 600

Company Secretary

Carr's Group plc

data.title: BRP Upgraded to Buy at Stifel Canada Following Fiscal Q1 Results; Price Target Raised to C$68.00
data.description:
Stifel Canada on Friday raised its rating on the shares of BRP to buy from hold while raising its price target to C$68.00 from C$49.00 following fiscal first-quarter results from the recreational vehicle manufacturer.

"BRP reported depressed Q1FY26 results with sales down 8% Y/Y, the seventh consecutive quarter of declining sales. However, this trend could end next quarter as the outlook is improving and Q2FY26 sales could increase Y/Y. Admittedly, this change in outlook arrives earlier than expected but is the catalyst that we have been waiting for to turn more positive. Our forecasts now call for revenues to increase Y/Y in each of the next three quarters and for EBITDA margins and EPS to increase in H2/FY26. BRP is expected to return to a growth mode, despite the industry still being depressed as the company has mostly completed its inventory depletion and will lap easier comparable periods. The risk of tariffs being a structural issue for BRP has also abated significantly. In addition, valuation is compelling at 6x forward EBITDA, a discount of 35% to Polaris forward consensus (PII, $39.89, NC). Hence, we are upgrading our rating to BUY," analyst Martin Landry wrote.

data.title: Descartes Systems Expected to Surpass Q1 Estimates Driven by 3GTMS Acquisition, RBC Says
data.description:
Descartes Systems Group is expected to report fiscal Q1 results ahead of consensus, driven by contributions from its recent $115 million acquisition of 3GTMS, RBC Capital Markets said in a note Friday.

The company is scheduled to report fiscal Q1 results on June 4.

RBC estimates revenue of $172 million, slightly above the $170 million consensus, with roughly $2.6 million in revenue attributed to one month of contribution from 3GTMS, which is not yet factored into consensus estimates.

Organic growth could also exceed expectations, supported by a rebound in global trade volumes and strong demand for Descartes' global trade intelligence solutions. US import volumes rose 11% year over year in March and 9% in April, up from 5% in February, the basis for Descartes' prior baseline outlook.

For fiscal Q2, the brokerage believes Descartes may provide a baseline implying results above consensus, reflecting the full-quarter impact of 3GTMS and continued strength in trade activity.

The 3GTMS acquisition, completed in March, marks Descartes' third-largest to date and highlights the company's continued focus on M&A as a growth strategy. The brokerage estimates the $115 million deal adds about $25 million in annual revenue.

RBC maintained an outperform rating on the stock with a $130 price target, noting the company's consistent capital deployment through acquisitions and organic growth

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1138 ET - End markets are too soft for BRP to see a good runway to growth. CIBC's Mark Petrie says in a report that the leisurecraft maker remains hurt by excess industry inventory and cautious consumer demand that skews away from its strengths. While management is trying to turn things around with new products and innovation, "we ultimately see a broader demand recovery as the key to more normalized margins and a healthier valuation, and believe this will take time." Meanwhile, tariffs remain a headwind, but these are easing, Petrie says. Still, looking at the broader market, peer inventory levels remain elevated, and non-current units continue to outperform because of the elevated discount programs in place. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1128 ET - The Fed's 5-year framework review, now underway, is expected to result in relatively narrow edits to how the Fed plans to respond to extended shortfalls in inflation. Some economists, including UT Austin's Carola Binder, pine for a deeper rethink of how the Fed steers the economy. Binder supports a shift to a nominal GDP target for the Fed--a regime where the Fed would set monetary policy not to target 2% inflation or low employment specifically, but to aim for a steady rate of nominal economic growth over time. That approach would also promote low inflation and unemployment, but would be simpler because it only includes one variable versus two, Binder argues, and it could make it easier for the Fed to explain what it's doing to the public, especially when inflation is low. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1113 ET - President Trump has taken an aggressive approach to lobbying Fed Chair Jerome Powell for lower interest rates, imploring him in speeches, private discussions and on social media to ease monetary policy. It's unusual for presidents to be so forthright, but it's not at all rare for elected officials in the U.S. and overseas to try to persuade central bankers to lower rates, University of Texas at Austin economics professor Carola Binder tells The Wall Street Journal. "Politicians want control over economic policy, and if you have a system to keep some of that control away from them, it's just designed to be contentious," Binder says. The wisdom of central-bank independence lies in removing the temptation for politicians to score quick electoral wins by meddling with rates, she says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1055 ET - Consumer spending grew at a modest 0.2% in April, as shoppers remained cautious about how tariff policy will affect the economy. But nominal incomes were up 0.8% month over month, a boost that allowed Americans to put a larger share of their income aside for the future, economists at Piper Sandler write. That will help keep household budgets healthy, priming spending to rise again if people begin feeling more confident about the economy. "Given solid incomes, if/when uncertainty around tariffs dissipates, consumers will be in a pretty good position to reaccelerate spending," Piper's Nancy Lazar writes to clients. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1006 ET - U.S. tariffs are more negative for the U.S. economy and for the dollar than for other countries and currencies, Bank of America analysts write. Tariffs create the risk of retaliation and the U.S. "trades more with the rest of the world than the rest of the world trades" with the U.S., they say. Data will determine the fate of the dollar. If U.S. indicators are positive, "we would expect investors to start ignoring the policy noise" and support the greenback. But BofA is bracing for bearish data, as policy uncertainty paralyzes hiring and investment plans and tariffs get higher. Fiscal policy loosening should result in higher borrowing costs, they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0943 ET - U.S. President Trump's tightening of immigration controls could present a headwind to digital assets, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. "For tech investors and digital asset markets--where the free movement of global talent is core--such moves might presage constraints in the future, impacting innovation cycles and digital asset flows." That comes amid Trump's expanding foray into cryptocurrencies, including the establishment of a strategic bitcoin reserve. Trump's efforts have improved bitcoin's mainstream adoption but also bring ethical and regulatory issues, he says. Bitcoin last trades down 0.6% at $105,578 after hitting a 10-day low of $104,648 earlier, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0901 ET - Dollar indexes are flat relative to yesterday's close as trade tensions gain momentum while a key U.S. inflation gauge inches closer to the Fed's target. Trump lashes out against China's trade practices, in a social media post. Meanwhile, April PCE inflation cools to 2.1% from 2.3%. The Fed targets 2%. However, markets keep pricing a hold on interest rates through September and a total of two cuts this year. Next week's labor report is likely to be scrutinized for signs of policy impact on employment. The dollar weakens 0.3% against the yen, while strengthening 0.2% to the euro.(paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0855 ET - Canada's GDP in 1Q rose at a faster-than-expected 2.2% annualized pace, or the fifth straight quarter of 2%-plus expansion. Yet, a key gauge turned negative, which the Bank of Canada will have to consider when it issues its next rate-policy decision and reflects broader weakness as tariff threats cast a pall over the economy. Final domestic demand declined 0.1%, or the first drop since late 2023. Business investment fell 3.1% in 1Q and government outlays dropped 0.3%. Those were offset by a 1.2% gain in consumer spending, although that marked a sharp slowdown from the previous quarter's 4.9% gain. Exports and inventories drove the 1Q gain, which Statistics Canada attributed to companies looking to sell and acquire goods before tariffs kicked in. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0843 ET - PCE inflation is slightly lower than expected, sparking a bonds selloff that sends Treasury yields higher. Annual inflation, by the Fed's preferred gauge, ticks lower to 2.1% in April from 2.3% in March. Economists surveyed by WSJ expected 2.2%. The core reading is 2.5%, down from 2.6%. Yields had been recovering from early lows after Trump expressed frustration, in social media, with China's trade practices. The inflation report accelerated the rise, keeping yields on track for a monthly gain. The 10-year is at 4.434% and the two-year at 3.939%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0836 ET - The first detailed look under the hood of Canada's economy since tariffs came into effect in early March at first blush suggests resilience, though there are cracks. GDP growth accelerated slightly to 2.2% annualized in 1Q, from downwardly revised growth of 2.1% the quarter before. That was stronger than the 1.7% expansion economists expected and 1.8% growth anticipated by the Bank of Canada. Growth was driven by exports, particularly autos and industrial machinery, which jumped in the early months of the year as U.S. importers looked to build inventories before tariffs kicked in. Offsetting that, imports were also up strongly, household spending cooled and home resale activity slumped. Business investment in non-residential structures was also down. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0815 ET - Equity markets held steady this week amid the latest trade developments and, with U.S. President Trump's tariffs in limbo, fatigue is evident, Barclays equity strategists say in a research note. While Trump's decision to push back a deadline on EU tariffs after a call with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen offered relief to markets, there are signs that calm is returning, the strategists say. "It also shows that investors are reacting more calmly to tariff headlines now, viewing them increasingly as negotiation tactics," they add. The Stoxx Europe 600 is up 0.2% and gained 0.7% since the start of the week. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0708 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.1% at $9,558.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.2% at $2,444.50 a ton. A stronger U.S. dollar is keeping downward pressure on metal prices, making it more expensive for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. Tariff uncertainty is also hitting metals demand, as a federal appeals court allowed U.S. President Trump's levies to stay in effect while the administration appeals a court decision that found them illegal. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has also said that trade talks with China are "a bit stalled" and may need direct contact between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, raising further concerns for metal demand. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Is spring still the best time to buy or sell a home?
data.description:
Key points:
  • 60% unsure if it's a good time to buy a home, BofA survey shows
  • Sales of new U.S. homes hit three-year high despite economic uncertainty
  • Expanded SALT cap could benefit big earners in high-tax states

This was originally published in the On The Money newsletter, where we share U.S. personal finance tips and insights every other week. Sign up here to receive it for free.

Spring traditionally is considered the buying and selling season for the housing market. But there has been a bit of a vibe shift. U.S. homeowners and prospective buyers are feeling uneasy, a new Bank of America survey shows, as rising mortgage rates deter buyers.

Of the 2,000 respondents to BofA's poll, 60% said they could not tell whether it was a good time to buy a home, according to the report published by the nation's second-largest lender. That is up from 57% last year and 48% in 2023.

Economic uncertainty is not stopping everyone from buying new homes: Sales of new U.S. single-family homes surged to more than a three-year high in April, as builders lowered prices to attract buyers. But new housing inventory is still near levels last seen in 2007.

New homes are not exactly cheap, either. The median new house price was $407,200 in April.

Another interesting tidbit is that new homes are getting smaller. So why aren’t they more affordable?

If you’re buying a home, selling a home or wondering what to do now, I want to hear from you. Tell me about your experience. Write to me at thomsonreuters.comonthemoney@thomsonreuters.com.

What the current SALT tax proposal means for you

I’ve been especially interested in tax legislation working its way through Congress, especially because the Republican-led U.S. House of Representatives passed a bill last week that would allow a deduction of up to $40,000 on federal returns for state and local taxes, known as SALT. A previous version of the bill had a cap of $30,000.

If passed by the Senate, the new expanded SALT cap would benefit millions of big earners in high-tax states, including New York (where I live), New Jersey, Pennsylvania (where my parents live), Maryland, Oregon and California.

Of course, bills tend to morph and change in the legislative process. As Donald Trump's so-called “big, beautiful bill” winds its way through the Senate, it could end up looking very different.

Follow the latest news here. And let me know if you stand to benefit from an expanded SALT deduction. Write to me at thomsonreuters.comonthemoney@thomsonreuters.com.

Read, watch and listen

What’s behind these big stock swings?

JetBlue, United tie up to let passengers book on both websites

Humanoid labor: Behind the global race for robot supremacy

How the student-loan crisis will show up in the economy (WSJ)

Trump administration axes Biden-era barrier for crypto in 401(k) plans (CNBC)

Why Amazon's in-car software deal with Stellantis fizzled


Retirees, get ready to need long-term care. Here’s what to know (NYT)

Five things I learned reading Reuters this week

  • According to LSEG, S&P 500 earnings are expected to increase 8.4% in 2025 compared with 12.1% in 2024. But the 2025 estimate is down sharply from 14% growth estimated on January 1.

  • In 2019, TikTok consumed, on average, over 2 minutes of each U.S. adult’s day. Five years later, that number increased to almost 18 minutes, according to an analysis by advisory firm Epyllion.

  • As Tesla models age and face rising competition from other electric vehicle makers, the cars now lose value faster than almost any other vehicle. An analysis by CarGurus, a used-car sales and data service, found that the average selling prices of used Teslas have fallen 7.6% over the last year, compared to a decline of 0.8% for a composite index of all brands. Prices for Tesla's Cybertruck have plunged 46% over the last year, while Model Y prices fell 14.1%.

  • The extended Line 14 of the Paris Metro now reaches Orly Airport, bringing the journey from the airport to the city center to under 20 minutes.

  • There were 5.5 working-age Americans for every over-65 in 1990. By 2023 that number had fallen below 4, according to World Bank data.

How do you stay informed about climate change?

Reuters is surveying readers like you to find out how you get your news about climate change, and what your concerns are around the topic. It took me less than two minutes to take the survey, andyou can do so here.

data.title: Broadcom Likely to Deliver Upside Fiscal Q2 Results, Q3 Outlook, Oppenheimer Says
data.description:
Broadcom will likely post upside fiscal Q2 results and a Q3 outlook next week with growth supported by its artificial intelligence computing and networking segments, Oppenheimer said in a note emailed Friday.

Broadcom's semiconductor segment is expected to increase 2% quarter on quarter and 10% year on year in Q2, while the company's AI chip business is expected to grow 7% quarter on quarter to $4.4 billion, the note said.

Oppenheimer highlighted the fact that Broadcom is the undisputed leader when it comes to the supply of custom Application-Specific Integrated Circuit, or ASIC, chips with seven announced projects.

The AI ASIC chip projects for Alphabet (GOOG), Meta Platforms (META) and ByteDance will be the main ones for the company in the near term, the note said.

Oppenheimer increased Broadcom's price target to $265 from $225, while reiterating the company's outperform rating.

data.title: Mullen Group Had its Outperform Rating Confirmed and Price Target Lowered by National Bank
data.description:
National Bank of Canada on Friday maintained its outperform rating on the shares of Mullen Group while trimming its price target lo $17.50 from $18.00 after the company closed its acquisition of Cole Group.

The bank said Mullen had $131 million in cash on hand and $7 million drawn on its available credit facility of $525 million. The company had also announced a new $400 million debt private placement, proceeds of which, will be used to prepay notes due in 2026.

"We therefore believe the company can make additional acquisitions this year and into 2026 as opportunities arise while still maintaining a comfortable financial position," said analyst Cameron Doerksen.

The bank now forecast Mullen's free cash flow at $150 million for an "attractive" free cash flow yield of about 12%, and sees its dividend yield as an "attractive" 5.9%.

"Although end markets remain challenged, Mullen is well positioned to grow through acquisition and valuation remains compelling," continued Doerksen.

data.title: The Summer Beauty Trends Worth Your Money — and the Ones to Skip — WSJ
data.description:
By Tatiana Boncompagni | Photographs by Elizabeth Coetzee/WSJ; Prop Styling by Jacqueline Draper

June is to beauty routines what March is to basketball: It's prime time, baby. "Summer brings a flood of new products as brands tap in to seasonal spending," said Marina Peredo, a dermatologist in New York. This time of year, knowing which, if any, of the vaunted beauty aids to actually buy can feel like trying to book a last-minute Disney vacation. Overwhelming. "Smart shopping helps you avoid gimmicks and save money — not everything is worth the hype." For advice on what is worth it — and what's best left on the shelf — read on.

Nailed It

Those status gel manicures that last a whole pay cycle (and put a dent in one too)? Turns out they're not all that great for your nail health. It's the removal process — not the application — that damages nails. "Prolonged acetone soaks will dehydrate the nail and cuticle, making the nail more prone to brittleness, peeling and breakage," said Dana Stern, a New York dermatologist.

This summer, reject traditional gel polishes and any formulas with toxic ingredients like formaldehyde, parabens and phthalates. Instead, reach for options that can be wiped off with an acetone-free remover. For a satiny, natural finish, we like the vegan Active Smooth line from Manucurist Paris. The shades provide translucent color and are formulated with plant fibers to fill in ridges.

If you're set on a gel polish, consider Dashing Diva. The brand's innovative remover will strip a shade in three minutes when you're ready for a color swap.

Another way to avoid damage? Shield your nails from both acetone and water. "Water dampens the nail which affects the longevity of the manicure," said manicurist Julie Kandalec. She prefers Lights Lacquer's Peel You Later Base Coat, a peel-off clear base layer made for quick, waterless removal.

For brittle or extra-weak nails, Stern advises avoiding polishes completely, and reaching instead for a treatment that mends the nail. The ePhD Bond Repair Nail Treatment from Epres is a great new oil-based take.

Ray of Hope

Many sunscreens charge for pretty packaging while offering only basic SPF. The best new options are loaded with skin-boosting ingredients plus de rigueur protection from UVA and UVB damage. A range of new formulas helps fight fine lines and acne, strengthen the skin barrier, combat rosacea and enhance your glow.

While Dendy Engelman, a New York cosmetic dermatologist, sees potential in the latest power players, she says that the wrong multi-hyphenate sunscreen can backfire. "Using a formula that's inappropriate for your skin could result in irritation, redness or breakouts," she warned.

A few of our favorite new contenders? Bliss Block Star Clarifying SPF, with salicylic acid and witch hazel to prevent breakouts, and Eighth Day's peptide-packed SPF-cum-primer to combat wrinkles. Rosacea sufferers might benefit from EltaMD UV Skin Recovery Red Color Correcting SPF, which neutralizes redness with a subtle green tint.

Face the Facts

You can thank South Korea for the newest treatment craze — salmon sperm (aka PDRN) facials. Applied topically after micro-needling or post-laser, the salmon-DNA compound purports to harness the body's own regenerative healing powers to give skin an antiaging boost.

But beyond fueling cocktail conversation, are salmon sperm facials worth it? Peredo says that PDRN (about $200 a vial in her office) can be an option for patients looking to recover quickly post dermatologic procedures, but it's not a miracle worker. Most important, Peredo says, PDRN is not FDA-cleared for injection in the U.S. The efficacy of over-the-counter PDRN creams is still anecdotal. We say: Throw this one back in the water until it matures.

To tap in to the wider regenerative skin care trend, Peredo suggests reaching instead for topical products with exosomes. Tiny vesicles made from human- or plant-derived stem cells, exosomes are the beauty industry's newest antiaging obsession. Exosomes pack the most punch when applied after in-office procedures like lasers, which increase absorption, says Peredo.

When applying an over-the-counter version at home, look for a formula that combines next-level regenerative additives like exosomes with tried-and-true ingredients. The Preservationist Vitamin C Cream Serum from Lenox and Sixteenth is formulated with ceramides, squalane, peptides and plant-derived exosomes. Skinfix's Barrier + Exo + Ectoin Intensive Repair Baume uses peptides and vegan biomimetic exosomes to smooth fine lines.

Tools of the Trade

A flurry of recent beauty-device launches promise to do everything but cook you a steak dinner. While plenty of impostors claim to erase fine lines and reduce inflammation (but don't), we're impressed by the 6-in-1 Skorr Glow skin care wand. The banana-sized, hand-held tool uses radio-frequency and LED among other technologies to drive products deeper into the skin and improve lymphatic drainage. It's "one of the most sophisticated options available," said Matthew White, a facial plastic surgeon in Manhattan.

Another good bet? Solawave's 4-in-1 Radiant Renewal Skincare Wand, which uses red light, heat, massage and microcurrent. These devices are best used to "maintain and boost the results" of professional treatments, advises White.

If you're looking to master a salon-quality blowout at home, consider the new wave of "intelligent" blow dryers that adjust heat, speed and air-flow settings to your specific hair type.

L'Oréal's new AirLight Pro is a good bet for frizz-prone, fine and curly hair, says Jae Manuel Cardenas, a New York hair stylist. Dyson's Supersonic Nural and the T3 Aire IQ — which automatically senses when you've set it down — also stood out in our research.

data.title: Dick's Sporting Goods Stock Is a Buy. Ignore the Shoe-Gazers. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Avi Salzman

Dick's Sporting Goods has struggled mightily this year. Its stock is set for a comeback.

Shares of Dick's have fallen 20% in 2025 as the national chain deals with tariffs and investor skepticism over its decision to buy struggling sneaker chain Foot Locker. The concept of a big-box store selling sports equipment might seem a little tired, too.

But don't throw out that old 9-iron just yet. Dick's has strung together a remarkable record of sales growth that makes the stock look like a bargain. And it's getting better at selling items online too. Its decision to buy Foot Locker adds some debt, which investors never like, but the rationale behind the pairing makes sense.

Start with Dick's earnings, which were reported late Wednesday. The company posted its fifth-straight quarter of same-store sales growth, a streak that puts most retailers to shame.

Its 4.5% same-store sales growth rate in the first quarter was driven largely by a boost in the size of the average customer purchase. If consumers are spending more of each paycheck at the stores, that bodes well for Dick's ability to navigate tariffs and other cost pressures. The company certainly thinks so. Dick's confirmed its earnings guidance for its current fiscal year, which ends in January, and even expects to widen its gross margins.

"Our business has a tremendous amount of momentum," said CEO Lauren Hobart.

Some analysts think the stock deserves a higher valuation given its consistent growth. Dick's now trades at 12 times 2026 earnings, in line with its historical average. Morgan Stanley's Simeon Gutman thinks the stock ought to trade at 15.5 times its expected 2026 earnings, which would lift it to $232, about 30% above its current level of around $180. "The stock's multiple has not broken out like it has for other retailers in our space, especially those successfully executing in e-commerce," Gutman wrote.

That's partially because of Foot Locker. The acquisition has been controversial because the sneaker chain has been struggling and its profit margins are much narrower than Dick's. But Dick's got a decent price, paying $2.4 billion for a company whose market value was well over $3 billion a year ago and whose annual sales are around $8 billion. It looks like it could be a classic turnaround story after the deal closes later this year.

The acquisition also allows Dick's to reach a new group of customers. Foot Locker's stores tend to be in malls or in urban locations where Dick's has very little presence. It also has stores overseas. Foot Locker's revenue has dwindled in part because of the chain's reliance on Nike, which makes up an estimated 60% of the company's sales. Though Nike had been emphasizing a direct-to-consumer strategy, it hasn't worked very well, and Dick's expects Nike to return to a wholesale strategy that will benefit Foot Locker.

"Dick's management emphasized that its relationship with Nike remains 'strategic,' expressing continued satisfaction with the partnership," writes Jefferies analyst Randal Konik. "This signals frequent collaboration and alignment between the two companies, reinforcing Nike's importance within Dick's merchandising strategy."

Forget the Foot Locker worries. Dick's can just do it.

Write to Avi Salzman at avi.salzman@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Consumer sentiment improves at the end of May following temporary trade truce with China
data.description:
A survey of consumer sentiment improved in late May on some signs that the tariff war with China might not be as damaging for the economy as feared.

The second of two readings of the consumer sentiment survey rose to 52.2 from 50.8 in early May, the University of Michigan said Friday. That matches the sentiment level in April.

Economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal had forecast a steady reading of 50.8.

This is breaking news. Check back for updates here.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Pitanium Shares Rise in Nasdaq Debut After Pricing IPO at $4 per Share
data.description:
Pitanium shares were gaining on their first day of trading on the Nasdaq exchange Friday.

The beauty and personal care products company said it priced its initial public offering of 1.75 million class A shares at $4 apiece.

A sole underwriter was granted a 45-day overallotment option to purchase up to 262,500 additional class A shares at the IPO price, according to a statement.

The offering is expected to close on June 2, and proceeds will be used to improve customer experience, build new products, explore new suppliers, ramp marketing strategies, and for working capital and general corporate purposes, the company said.

data.title: REG - Unite Group PLC - Total Voting Rights
data.description:
RNS Number : 8647K Unite Group PLC (The) 30 May 2025  

THE UNITE GROUP PLC (the "Company")

30 May 2025

TOTAL VOTING RIGHTS AND SHARE CAPITAL

In accordance with the Disclosure and Transparency Rules (DTR 5.6), the Company makes the following disclosure with respect to the share capital and voting rights of the Company.

As at 30 May 2025, the capital of the Company consists of 489,352,205 ordinary shares with voting rights. The Company does not hold any shares in Treasury.

Therefore, the total number of shares in the Company is 489,352,205.

The above figure (489,352,205) may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the FCA's Disclosure and Transparency Rules.

END

data.title: Increased number of shares and votes in Yubico
data.description:
The number of shares and votes in Yubico AB (the “company”) has, during May 2025, increased as a result of exercise of warrants under the company’s incentive program, for subscription of a total of 161,164 ordinary shares, and as a result of the board of directors’ resolution to issue and repurchase a total of 865,000 class C shares pursuant to the authorization granted by the annual general meeting on May 13, 2025. The share issue and the repurchase have been carried out under the company’s long term incentive program, which was resolved upon by the annual general meeting.

Today, the last trading day of the month, there are a total of 87,291,882 shares in the company, of which 86,426,882 ordinary shares and 865,000 class C shares, and as of the same date the number of votes amounts to 86,513,382.

data.title: Change in the number of shares and votes in Proact IT Group AB (publ)
data.description:
In September, Proact IT Group AB cancelled 300,000 shares, in accordance with the authorisation granted by the Annual General Meeting on 6 May 2025. The cancelled shares correspond to 1.09 percent of the company's shares and votes.

The total number of shares and votes in Proact IT Group AB as of 30 May 2025 thus amounts to 27,101,658. After the cancellation, Proact IT Group AB holds 428,689 own shares, which corresponds to 1.58 percent of the company's shares and votes.

RNS Number : 8052K Vanquis Banking Group PLC 30 May 2025  

Vanquis Banking Group plc - Voting Rights and Capital

In accordance with the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rule 5.6.1., Vanquis Banking Group plc hereby gives notice that as of 30 May 2025, the company's issued share capital consists of 256,482,888 ordinary shares of 20 8/11p each, with voting rights. Vanquis Banking Group plc does not hold any ordinary shares in Treasury.

The above figure 256,482,888 may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, Vanquis Banking Group plc under the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

data.title: Fannie Mae Announces Results of Tender Offer for Any and All of Certain CAS Notes
data.description:
WASHINGTON, May 30, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Fannie Mae today announced the results of its fixed-price cash tender offers (each, an "Offer" and, collectively, the "Offers") for any and all of certain Connecticut Avenue Securities(R) (CAS) Notes listed in the table below (the "Notes") upon the terms and subject to the conditions set forth in the Offer to Purchase and related Notice of Guaranteed Delivery, each dated as of May 22, 2025 (collectively, the "Offer Documents"). Certain of the classes of Notes subject to the Offers were issued by the REMIC trusts identified in the table below (each, a "Trust"). Fannie Mae is the holder of the ownership certificate issued by each Trust and, as a result, the sole beneficial owner of each Trust.

A total of approximately $1,798 million in original principal amount of Notes were validly tendered and not validly withdrawn on or before the designated expiration time for the Offers, which was 5:00 PM New York City time on May 29, 2025. The table below sets forth the original principal balance of the Notes, the percentage of original principal amount tendered, and the original principal amount tendered in the Offers.

tender initiated during the tender offer period. 
(2) Rounded to the nearest hundredth of a percent.

using the Notice of Guaranteed Delivery.

The settlement date for the Notes tendered and accepted for purchase in the Offers is expected to occur on June 2, 2025 (the "Settlement Date"). Any Notes tendered and accepted for purchase in the Offers using the Notice of Guaranteed Delivery are expected to be purchased on June 3, 2025.

BofA Securities acted as the designated lead dealer manager and Wells Fargo Securities acted as the designated dealer manager for the Offers. Global Bondholder Services Corporation was engaged as the tender agent and information agent for the Offers.
